parts.content: Explain how new species can arise through speciation.
parts.markscheme:
- population becomes reproductively isolated <marks>1 mark</marks>
- no gene flow occurs between subgroups <marks>1 mark</marks>
- isolation may be geographic (allopatric) or without geographic separation (sympatric) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- different selection pressures in different environments drive divergence <marks>1 mark</marks>
- mutations accumulate independently in separated populations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- genetic drift affects isolated populations (especially small populations) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- pre-zygotic isolation mechanisms can evolve (e.g. behavioural/temporal/ecological isolation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- post-zygotic isolation mechanisms can evolve (hybrid inviability/sterility) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- eventually subgroups cannot interbreed to produce fertile offspring <marks>1 mark</marks>
- formation of a new species = speciation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- example: finches on the Galápagos Islands <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 7 marks</note>

parts.content: Outline barriers to gene flow that can contribute to reproductive isolation between populations.
parts.markscheme:
- Reproductive isolation reduces or prevents gene flow between populations/species <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Prezygotic barriers prevent mating or fertilization (e.g. temporal, ecological/habitat, behavioural, mechanical, gametic isolation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Postzygotic barriers reduce hybrid viability or fertility (e.g. hybrid inviability, hybrid sterility) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Barriers can be strengthened by natural selection/reinforcement when hybrids have reduced fitness <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how hybridization can influence speciation, including at least one mechanism by which a new species may arise.
parts.markscheme:
- Hybridization introduces gene flow between diverging populations and can oppose divergence by mixing alleles <marks>1 mark</marks>
- If hybrids have lower fitness, selection favours assortative mating/prezygotic isolation (reinforcement), increasing reproductive isolation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- If hybrids have similar/high fitness, populations may fuse/merge, reducing or preventing speciation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Hybrids may persist in a stable hybrid zone if there is a balance between dispersal of parent populations and selection against/for hybrids in the contact region <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A new species can arise via hybrid speciation when hybrids become reproductively isolated from both parent populations <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the implications of hybrid zones for speciation, including potential outcomes for the populations involved.
parts.markscheme:
- Hybrid zones are geographic regions where genetically distinct populations meet and interbreed, producing hybrid offspring <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They provide evidence about the strength/nature of reproductive isolation and the permeability of species boundaries (extent of gene flow) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- If hybrids have reduced fitness, selection favours traits that reduce interbreeding, strengthening prezygotic isolation (reinforcement) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reinforcement can increase divergence and promote completion of speciation between the populations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- If hybrids have equal or greater fitness than parent types, gene pools may merge (fusion), reducing divergence <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Hybrid zones can remain stable if hybrids are favoured only in the intermediate environment or if there is a balance between dispersal and selection (tension zone) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Hybridization can generate novel allele combinations and, if reproductive isolation subsequently evolves, contribute to formation of a new species (hybrid speciation) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award full marks only if at least three potential outcomes are explained with clear links to hybrid fitness and gene flow.</note>

steps.title: Analyze the Request
steps.content:
The question asks us to explain how molecular evidence supports the theory of evolution. This specifically relates to **Topic A4.1: Evolution and speciation** and **Topic A3.2: Classification and cladistics**.

We need to discuss how analyzing DNA and proteins helps us understand evolutionary relationships and construct phylogenetic trees.

steps.title: Sequence Similarity
steps.content:
The primary form of molecular evidence comes from comparing the **base sequences of DNA** or the **amino acid sequences of proteins** between different organisms.

When we compare these sequences, we find striking similarities between distinct species. For example, humans and chimpanzees share a very high percentage of their DNA sequence.

steps.title: Indication of Common Ancestry
steps.content:
These molecular similarities are not coincidental; they provide strong evidence for **common ancestry**. 

The logic is simple: species have similar genetic codes because they inherited them from a shared ancestor. Over time, as populations separate and evolve, mutations occur, but the core sequence remains similar.

steps.title: Constructing Phylogenetic Trees
steps.content:
Scientists use this molecular data to construct **phylogenetic trees** (or cladograms). 

These are diagrams that map out the evolutionary pathways, showing which species are most closely related. Species that are grouped together on the same branch (clade) share a more recent common ancestor than those on distant branches.

steps.title: The Molecular Clock
steps.content:
Molecular evidence also acts as a timeline. Mutations tend to accumulate in DNA at a relatively constant rate over long periods. 

This means that the **number of differences** in sequences can be used to estimate **divergence times**—how long ago two species split from a common ancestor. 

- **Few differences:** The species diverged recently.
- **Many differences:** The species diverged a long time ago.

steps.title: Supporting Other Evidence
steps.content:
Finally, molecular evidence is powerful because it often confirms what we have learned from other sources, such as **fossils** and **homologous structures** (morphology).

When DNA analysis matches the evolutionary trees built from fossil records, it strengthens the overall theory of evolution significantly.

parts.content: Outline the role of reproductive isolation in the formation of new species.
parts.markscheme:
- Reproductive isolation reduces/prevents gene flow between populations. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Prevented gene flow allows populations to diverge genetically (e.g. via mutation, natural selection, genetic drift). <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Over time, differences accumulate so that individuals can no longer interbreed to produce fertile offspring (new species formed). <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Distinguish between allopatric and sympatric speciation.
parts.markscheme:
- Allopatric speciation occurs when populations become geographically separated/isolated. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Physical/geographic barrier prevents gene flow between the separated populations (allopatric). <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Sympatric speciation occurs within the same geographical area (no physical separation). <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Gene flow is reduced/prevented by ecological/behavioral/temporal isolation or genetic changes such as polyploidy (sympatric). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain allopatric and sympatric speciation, including one named example of each.
parts.markscheme:
- Both mechanisms result in the formation of new species (speciation). <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Speciation requires reproductive isolation/prevention of gene flow between populations. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Allopatric speciation: populations become geographically separated so gene flow is prevented and populations diverge under different selection pressures/drift. <marks>2 marks</marks>

- Example of allopatric speciation: Galápagos finches evolving on different islands, with adaptation to different food sources leading to differences (e.g. beak size/shape). <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Sympatric speciation: occurs in the same geographical area when gene flow is reduced by ecological/behavioral isolation or genetic mechanisms (e.g. polyploidy). <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Example of sympatric speciation: apple maggot fly (*Rhagoletis pomonella*) diverging due to host plant preference. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for other valid examples and comparisons</note>

questions.specification:
A population of crop-raiding insects was treated with a newly developed insecticide. The table shows the percentage of the population that survived the application of the insecticide over four consecutive years.

| Year | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|

options.content: Individual insects became resistant to the toxin during their lifetime.
options.markscheme: Physiological resistance acquired during an individual's lifetime is not heritable, so it cannot explain a multi-year population-level increase.

options.content: The insecticide induced mutations that produced resistance alleles in offspring.
options.markscheme: While mutations can occur, the consistent increase is best explained by selection acting on existing variation rather than the insecticide acting as a directed mutagen.

options.content: Alleles conferring resistance increased in frequency in the population.
options.markscheme: Insecticide acts as a selection pressure; resistant individuals survive and reproduce, increasing resistance allele frequency over generations.

options.content: Insects learned to avoid the insecticide through social behaviour.
options.markscheme: Learned avoidance is not a genetic change and is unlikely to account for a sustained generational increase in survival percentage.

parts.content: Using the table, describe how reproductive isolation changes with genetic divergence in sympatric and allopatric populations.
parts.markscheme:
- Reproductive isolation increases with genetic divergence in both sympatric and allopatric populations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- At the same genetic divergence, sympatric populations show greater reproductive isolation than allopatric populations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The gap between sympatric and allopatric isolation is large at low–intermediate divergence and narrows at higher divergence (both approach high isolation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sympatric isolation rises more steeply (reaches high isolation at lower genetic divergence) than allopatric isolation <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how sympatric speciation can occur despite the lack of geographical separation, and give one biological factor that may contribute to it.
parts.markscheme:
- Sympatric speciation occurs in the same geographic area when reproductive isolation evolves within a population due to reduced gene flow between subpopulations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- One factor that can promote sympatric speciation:

polyploidy <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

sexual selection (assortative mating) <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

niche specialization / ecological differentiation (e.g., host shift) <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

behavioral isolation (e.g., differences in courtship signals) <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

temporal isolation (different breeding times) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify the genetic divergence at which the difference in reproductive isolation between sympatric and allopatric speciation is greatest. Suggest a reason for this difference.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate differences (sympatric minus allopatric): 0.1 → $42-8=34$; 0.2 → $68-18=50$; 0.3 → $82-30=52$; 0.4 → $90-45=45$; 0.5 → $94-60=34$; 0.6 → $97-72=25$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Greatest difference occurs at genetic divergence 0.3 (difference $=52\%$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Suggestion: reinforcement in sympatry (selection against unfit hybrids increases pre-zygotic isolation more rapidly than in allopatry) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allopatric populations have less opportunity for selection against hybrids because they do not regularly encounter and mate with the other population until secondary contact <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline the process of natural selection leading to the evolution of antibiotic resistance in a population of bacteria.
parts.markscheme:
- Genetic variation exists within the bacterial population due to random mutations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Some mutations confer resistance to a specific antibiotic <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The application of the antibiotic acts as a selective pressure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Susceptible bacteria are killed, while resistant individuals survive <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Resistant bacteria reproduce / undergo binary fission <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Resistance alleles are passed on to the next generation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The frequency of the resistant phenotype increases within the population over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>*Award maximum 4 marks*</note>

parts.content: Explain the mechanisms of allopatric and sympatric speciation and how they lead to the divergence of species.
parts.markscheme:
- Speciation is the process by which one species splits into two or more distinct species <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allopatric speciation occurs when populations are geographically isolated by physical barriers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Examples include mountain ranges, oceans, or deserts <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Geographic isolation prevents gene flow between the two populations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Each population is subject to different selective pressures / environmental conditions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sympatric speciation occurs within the same geographical area / without physical separation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Behavioral isolation can occur if mating rituals or courtship songs change <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Temporal isolation occurs if populations begin breeding at different times / seasons <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ecological isolation occurs if populations occupy different niches in the same habitat <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Genetic drift can cause further divergence, especially in small populations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Divergence eventually leads to the inability of the groups to produce fertile offspring <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>*Award maximum 7 marks*</note>

parts.content: Using the pentadactyl limb as an example, explain how homologous structures provide evidence for evolution.
parts.markscheme:
- Homologous structures are anatomical features that share a similar basic structure but perform different functions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The pentadactyl limb consists of a specific arrangement of bones (humerus, radius/ulna, carpals, and five digits) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This shared structure across mammals, birds, reptiles, and amphibians suggests a common ancestor <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Differences in the limbs are adaptations for different environments / modes of life (e.g., flight, swimming, or running) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This pattern of diversification from a common structure is known as adaptive radiation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The greater the similarity in structure, the more closely related the species are likely to be <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>*Award maximum 4 marks*</note>

parts.content: Outline the relationship between an enzyme's active site and its substrate specificity.
parts.markscheme:
- The active site is a specific region/pocket on the surface of the enzyme. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The shape and chemical properties of the active site are complementary to a specific substrate. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Complementary binding allows formation of an enzyme–substrate complex (lock-and-key or induced-fit). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Binding positions the substrate(s) correctly and lowers activation energy, so only the correct substrate is catalysed efficiently (basis of specificity). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the industrial process of producing lactose-free milk using immobilized enzymes.
parts.markscheme:
- Lactase is obtained from yeast/bacteria/fungi. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The enzyme is immobilized by attachment to an inert substance/alginate beads. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Milk is passed through/over the immobilized lactase. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lactose is hydrolyzed/broken down into glucose and galactose. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The immobilized enzyme is not lost/is recovered for reuse. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Prevents contamination of the final product with the enzyme. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allows for a continuous production process. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Accept other valid benefits of enzyme immobilization such as increased thermal stability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the effects of temperature, pH, and substrate concentration on the rate of enzyme activity.
parts.markscheme:
- As temperature increases, kinetic energy increases, leading to more frequent collisions between enzymes and substrates. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reaction rate increases until an optimum temperature is reached. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- At very high temperatures, enzymes denature/tertiary structure is altered. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Denaturation is usually irreversible and stops the reaction. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enzymes have an optimum pH where activity is highest. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Extreme pH changes can break ionic/hydrogen bonds and cause denaturation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- As substrate concentration increases, the rate of reaction increases due to more frequent collisions with active sites. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- At high substrate concentrations, the rate plateaus/reaches a maximum ($V_{max}$) because all active sites are occupied/saturated. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Different enzymes have different optima depending on their environment (e.g., pepsin in stomach). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe one additional mechanism by which antibiotic resistance can spread in bacteria.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks> for one mechanism described clearly. Examples include:

- Conjugation: transfer of plasmids carrying antibiotic-resistance genes via a pilus between bacterial cells <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Resistance gene is on an R-plasmid / plasmid is replicated and transferred to recipient cell <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recipient cell expresses the resistance gene and survives antibiotic exposure, increasing frequency of resistance in the population <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Transformation: uptake of free DNA containing a resistance gene from the environment <marks>1 mark</marks>
- DNA is integrated into the bacterial genome/plasmid <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transformed bacterium expresses resistance and survives selection by antibiotic <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Transduction: bacteriophage transfers resistance gene between bacteria <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Phage packages bacterial DNA including resistance gene and infects another bacterium <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Newly infected bacterium gains resistance and is selected for in presence of antibiotic <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept other valid mechanisms, e.g. selection of pre-existing resistant variants, provided the spread mechanism beyond vertical inheritance is described.</note>

parts.content: Outline the process of speciation.
parts.markscheme:
- Speciation is the formation of new species. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Results from reproductive isolation of populations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Genetic divergence leads to differences in phenotypes/genotypes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Prevents interbreeding between populations (even if they come back into contact). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Accumulation of mutations/differences in allele frequencies over time. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- May occur via geographic separation (allopatric) or within the same area (sympatric). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Leads to the development of unique gene pools. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 4 marks</note>

parts.content: Describe two types of reproductive isolation.
parts.markscheme:
- Temporal isolation: populations breed at different times/seasons. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Behavioral isolation: populations have different mating calls/rituals/courtship behaviors. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mechanical isolation: incompatible reproductive anatomy. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Gametic isolation: sperm/pollen cannot fertilize the egg. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Hybrid sterility: offspring (hybrids) are produced but cannot reproduce. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Habitat/ecological isolation: populations live in different environments/niches within the same area. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Each barrier prevents gene flow between populations. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for naming the type and 1 mark for a brief description, up to a maximum of 4 marks.</note>

parts.content: Explain how natural selection can lead to evolution.
parts.markscheme:
- Populations show variation due to mutation/meiosis/sexual reproduction. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- More individuals are born than the environment can support/overproduction of offspring. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Individuals with favorable traits/adaptations are better suited to the environment and survive better. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Survivors reproduce and pass on their favorable alleles/traits to offspring. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allele frequency changes in the population over generations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Adaptation increases the overall fitness of the population. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Leads to evolutionary change/speciation over long periods. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Selection pressure (e.g. predation, disease, climate) drives differential survival. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Characteristics of the population gradually change. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 7 marks</note>

options.content: The environmental stress of the drought caused individual birds to physically modify their beaks to suit the harder seeds.
options.markscheme: This is a Lamarckian misconception. Individuals do not adapt their physical structures during their lifetime to suit the environment; instead, evolutionary change occurs at the population level as those with favorable traits survive and reproduce.

options.content: Natural selection favored individuals with larger beak depths due to their increased feeding efficiency.
options.markscheme: Natural selection occurs when environmental changes (the drought) provide a selective advantage to individuals with specific variations (larger beak depths). The data shows that birds with larger beaks had a significantly higher survival rate because they could crack the remaining large seeds, leading to a shift in the population's characteristics.

options.content: The drought increased the frequency of mutation events in the population, resulting in new alleles for increased beak size.
options.markscheme: Mutations are random genetic events and are not 'triggered' or directed by the environmental needs of an organism.

options.content: Survival was independent of beak size, and the observed change was due to a random founder effect.
options.markscheme: The data clearly shows that survival was not random; there is a strong correlation between beak depth and the probability of survival, which is characteristic of natural selection rather than random genetic drift or the founder effect.

parts.content: Explain how a population of bacteria can evolve resistance to an antibiotic and discuss the factors that could cause this change to happen rapidly.
parts.markscheme:
- evolution involves a change in the frequency of alleles in the bacterial population over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- genetic variation arises through spontaneous mutations (or horizontal gene transfer/plasmids) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- the antibiotic acts as a strong selection pressure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- bacteria with resistance alleles have a selective advantage/higher fitness in the presence of the antibiotic <marks>1 mark</marks>
- surviving bacteria reproduce (binary fission) and pass the resistance alleles to their offspring/the next generation <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Factors for rapid evolution:**

- high frequency/misuse of antibiotics providing constant selection pressure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- short generation time of bacteria allows for rapid turnover of populations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- horizontal gene transfer (via conjugation/transformation) allows for the rapid spread of resistance genes between individuals/species <marks>1 mark</marks>
- high mutation rates in some bacterial strains <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 4 marks; maximum 3 marks if no factors for rapid evolution are provided</note>

parts.content: Haemophilia A is a blood-clotting disorder that occurs more frequently in males than females. Describe the inheritance pattern of this condition.
parts.markscheme:
- it is a sex-linked trait/the gene is located on the non-homologous region of the X chromosome <marks>1 mark</marks>
- the allele for haemophilia is recessive to the normal clotting allele <marks>1 mark</marks>
- females can be carriers (heterozygous) without showing symptoms <marks>1 mark</marks>
- males are more likely to be affected because they only have one X chromosome/are hemizygous (so a single recessive allele causes the disorder) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- affected fathers cannot pass the trait to their sons (as they pass only the Y chromosome to sons) <marks>1 mark</marks>
